docs.title: Credit Transfer Portfolio
docs.text:
Applicant: Shanshan Zhong (SYSU Master, ID 22214624) → CMU PhD program (szhong2)

This site documents 6 credit-transfer requests mapping graduate-level courses I took at Sun Yat-sen University (SYSU) to courses at Carnegie Mellon University (CMU). Each request provides exactly the three parts:

  1. a public URL to the CMU course syllabus
  2. a lecture-by-lecture mapping with a quantified ≥ 75 % coverage check
  3. a homework comparison between the two courses

docs.title: Request overview
docs.text:
All SYSU courses are 3-credit graduate courses (54 class periods of 45 min each, over an 18-week semester). Grades are on a 0–100 scale; passing = 60.

# SYSU Course CMU Course Grade 1 Advanced Computer Networks 15-641 Networking and the Internet 92 2 Pattern Recognition + Computational Visual Media 11-777 Multimodal Machine Learning 88 / 90 3 Stochastic Processes 15-857 Analytical Performance Modeling 85 4 Advanced Algorithms + Machine Learning 10-601 Introduction to Machine Learning 88 / 85 5 Advanced Algorithms and Programming Techniques 15-451/651 Algorithm Design and Analysis 88 6 Machine Learning 10-703 Deep Reinforcement Learning and Control 85

Requests 5 & 6 are deprecated

  • Request 5 (Algorithms → 15-651) is superseded by Request 4 (which combines Algorithms + ML into a two-for-one mapping against CMU 10-601).
  • Request 6 (ML → 10-703) scored below 75 % and is kept for reference only. Neither should be submitted.

Changes from v1 (in response to Prof. Jamie Callan's 23 Jan 2026 review)

The original (v1) credit-transfer petition mapped courses to CMU targets that Prof. Callan correctly identified as poor fits. This v2 dossier re-targets those requests and reorganises the numbering after deeper lecture-by-lecture analysis:

Re-targeted CMU courses:

v1 target v2 target Reason for change 15-750 Algorithms in the Real World 10-601 Introduction to Machine Learning (Request 4, two-for-one: Algo + ML) 15-750 is CMU's advanced electives course (SVD, cache-oblivious algorithms, etc.) — a poor match. The SYSU Algorithms course alone mapped to 15-451/651 at only 66.67 %, below the 75 % threshold. By combining it with the SYSU ML course, the joint coverage of 10-601 reaches 81.48 %. 10-701 Introduction to Machine Learning 10-601 Introduction to Machine Learning (same Request 4) v1 mapped SYSU ML alone to 10-701; v2 initially re-targeted to 10-703 (Deep RL) but that scored only 59.62 %. The ML course is now combined with Algorithms in the two-for-one Request 4 against 10-601.

docs.title: Coverage at a glance
docs.text:
Scoring model. Each row in a Lecture Alignment page corresponds to one CMU lecture. A lecture counts as covered iff SYSU has actually taught its content:

Badge Score Meaning Fully Covered 1.00 The lecture is taught at SYSU Mostly Covered 0.75 Main content taught; minor details may be missing Partially Covered 0.50 Roughly half of the lecture's material is taught Conceptually Related 0.25 Only conceptually related; not actually taught Indirectly Supported 0.00 Only theoretical prerequisites are present \[ \text{Coverage} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{\text{lecture}} \text{score}}{N_{\text{CMU lectures}}} \qquad \text{Pass threshold} = 75\%. \]

scripts/compute_coverage.py parses the badges and rewrites the bars below.

docs.title: Request 1: Lecture Alignment
docs.text:
SYSU Advanced Computer Networks ▶ CMU 15-641 Networking and the Internet

  • CMU — 15-641 Networking and the Internet

    Focus. Introductory course in computer networks. Emphasis on the basic performance and engineering trade-offs in the design and implementation of computer networks. Includes several multi-week projects requiring significant design and implementation.

    Objectives. Students learn not only what computer networks are and how they work today, but also why they are designed the way they are and how they are likely to evolve in the future.

    Topics: congestion / flow / error control, routing, addressing, naming, multicasting, switching, internetworking, and network security. Evaluation is based on homework assignments, projects, one midterm exam, and one final exam.

  • SYSU — Advanced Computer Networks

    Focus. Graduate-level introduction to modern computer networking: Internet architecture, packet switching, performance modeling, multimedia networking and QoS, network security, SDN / OpenFlow / P4, NFV / middleboxes, wireless and 5G systems, IPv6 / SRv6. Emphasis on emerging Internet architectures, programmable networking, wireless / mobile systems, and security protocols used in real deployments.

    Objectives. Develop an analytical understanding of QoS and multimedia networking, cryptographic protocols (TLS / IPsec), SDN and P4 programmable data planes, and wireless / cellular systems. Conduct a research-style final group project that culminates in an applied network-security artifact (e.g., Ethereum P2P / cryptographic protocols).

Coverage: 79.55% ≥ 75% 🟩 Full 11 · 🟩 Mostly 7 · 🟨 Partial 2 · ⬜ Conceptual 1 · ⬛ Indirect 1 (n=22)

CMU lecture names below are taken verbatim from the official CMU 15-441/641 Fall 2024 schedule; each lecture title is itself a clickable link to the corresponding slide deck on Prof. Steenkiste's Google Drive. SYSU evidence cites the four slide decks (Nn s.X = deck n, slide X, click to jump to that slide) and the final group project (an applied network-security research artifact on Ethereum P2P / cryptographic protocols).

Legend: Fully (1.00) · Mostly (0.75) · Partial (0.50) · Conceptual (0.25) · Indirect (0.00)

Real gap. L11 Tue 10/1 Advanced Congestion Control Conceptual N3 traffic-engineering systems (Google B4) provide motivation; ECN / BBR / DCTCP not taught L12 Thu 10/3 QoS, Rate Limit, and Performance Fully N1 s55-59 explicit QoS chapter opener: bandwidth, latency, jitter, packet-loss, control granularity (packet/flow/session), per-flow vs flow-aggregate; N1 s124-131 packet classifier, marking, policing, isolation, scheduling (Round Robin, WFQ); N1 s137 token-bucket edge marking; N1 s142 call admission / per-connection QoS guarantees L13 Tue 10/22 Domain Name System (DNS) Mostly N1 s74-78 full DNS resolution sequence in CDN lookup workflow: local DNS → netcinema.com authoritative DNS → KingCDN authoritative DNS → CNAME redirection → nearby CDN edge node. L14 Thu 10/24 HTTP and Web Performance Mostly N1 s60-66 continuous-playout constraint, jitter, client-side buffering, then N1 s68 streaming over HTTP (TCP-based, fill-rate fluctuation, firewall traversal); N1 s68-73 DASH client adaptation logic; N1 s77 OTT challenges. HTTP HOL-blocking specifics not directly named L15 Tue 10/29 Content Distribution Fully N1 s74-79 CDN architecture (deep vs bring-home), Akamai, KingCDN end-to-end lookup example, OTT challenges, Netflix case study with manifest file + DASH server selection L16 Thu 10/31 Video on Demand Fully N1 s60-66 streaming stored video, playout buffer, client-side buffering for jitter compensation, constant-bit-rate transmission analysis; N1 s68-73 DASH adaptive bitrate chunk selection L17 Thu 11/7 Datacenter Networks Partial N3 s114-118 Google B4 inter-datacenter traffic engineering: max-min fair-share, LP-vs-approximation, 99% bandwidth utilisation; N3 s132 China Mobile Novonet 5G core-network NFV deployment (largest cloud network). intra-DC topology (fa24 reading: "A Scalable, Commodity Datacenter Network Architecture" — fat-tree / Clos) less covered L18 Tue 11/12 Security I: Cryptography Basics Fully N2 s9-30 language of cryptography, brute-force vs known-plaintext attacks, symmetric crypto, substitution ciphers; N2 s40-79 authentication protocols ap1.0 → ap5.0 (nonce, public-key); N2 s80-110 full TLS handshake + IPsec (AH/ESP, transport vs tunnel mode, SAD/SPD); Final project Part 3-4 ECDH key exchange + ECDSA full algorithm derivation — Thu 11/14 Security II: Routing Security Mostly N3 s92-95 BGP shortcomings (not capacity-aware, not performance-aware) framed as research-grade routing-security concerns; Final project Part 2 applied research on routing attacks in Ethereum P2P: eclipse attacks, prefix hijack, botnet attacks, GNN-based detection, secondary verification defense. RPKI / BGPsec not by name but the underlying threat model is fully covered L19 Tue 11/19 Wireless 1: PHY and WiFi Fully N4 s9-30 wireless link characteristics: path loss, interference, SNR–BER, hidden / exposed terminals; N4 s35-44 802.11 MAC: CSMA/CA, link-layer ACK, RTS/CTS; N4 s50-53 802.11 frame format and addressing L20 Thu 11/21 Wireless: Cellular Fully N4 s58-65 4G/5G architecture opener; N4 s65-75 LTE functional split: control / user-plane separation, eNodeB ↔ S-GW ↔ P-GW, GTP bearer tunnels; N4 s78-88 5G NR / 5G core, 4G LTE authentication & encryption, 4G→5G security evolution; N4 s116 Mobile IP (RFC 5944) architecture L21 Tue 12/3 Network Functions Fully N2 s125-138 firewalls (stateless / stateful packet filters), application gateways, IDS, IP-spoofing limitations; N3 s132-137 middleboxes, NFV, China Mobile NFV deployment, evolution from proprietary appliances to whitebox hardware + match+action — both pillars of 15-641 L21 (security NFs + virtualised NFs) fully covered

docs.title: Notes on coverage
docs.text:
• The 18 fully or largely covered lectures span the complete CMU 15-641 core: performance, OSI / IP layering, routing (DV + LS + BGP + SDN), QoS, DNS, HTTP / CDN / VoD, the entire security stack (crypto, TLS, IPsec, firewalls / NFV), and all wireless / cellular content.

  • The 2 partial lectures cover topics that SYSU introduces but does not derive in depth (Ethernet's CSMA/CD, intra-DC Clos topology).

  • Only TCP windowing (L10) and advanced congestion control (L11) are genuinely thin on the SYSU side, as expected for a graduate-level course that re-frames classical mechanisms via SDN / traffic-engineering perspectives.

  • The final group project is research-grade applied network security (Ethereum P2P, ECDH key exchange, ECDSA derivation, applied routing-attack analysis) that exceeds the scope of a typical 15-641 homework, providing strong evidence for the cryptography and routing-security rows.

docs.title: Request 2: Syllabuses
docs.text:
Two SYSU courses → one CMU course

SYSU Pattern Recognition (PR) and Computational Visual Media (CVM) were designed as a consecutive sequence forming a single graduate track on multimodal learning. PR provides the multimodal foundation; CVM focuses on visual–language integration and multimodal generation. Together they cover the six MMML challenges (representation, alignment, reasoning, generation, transference, quantification) that CMU 11-777 is built around. Total credit: 3 + 3 = 6 credits / 108 contact hours.

docs.title: Request 2: Lecture Alignment
docs.text:
SYSU Pattern Recognition + Computational Visual Media ▶ CMU 11-777 Multimodal Machine Learning

  • CMU — 11-777 Multimodal Machine Learning

    Focus. Presents the fundamental mathematical concepts in machine learning and deep learning relevant to the six main challenges in multimodal machine learning: (1) representation, (2) alignment, (3) reasoning, (4) generation, (5) transference, and (6) quantification. Includes multimodal transformers, neuro-symbolic models, multimodal tensor fusion, mutual information, and multimodal graph networks. Discusses recent applications of MMML including multimodal affect recognition, multimodal language grounding, and language–vision navigation.

    Objectives. Equip students to design, implement, and evaluate multimodal models that combine vision, language, audio, and structured modalities; reason about cross-modal representation, alignment, and generation; and complete a CVPR-style team research project culminating in a final report and presentation.

  • SYSU — Pattern Recognition (PR) + Computational Visual Media (CVM)

    Focus.

    • PR. Foundations of statistical pattern recognition (Bayesian theory, classifiers, neural networks, HMMs, deep models), representation and compression (dimensionality reduction, model compression), and applications in NLP, image/video understanding, and recommender systems.
    • CVM. Geometry- and filtering-based imaging, video understanding / visualization (structure analysis, summarization, retrieval), and sketch computing (data, understanding, applications).
    • Combined. Emphasizes vision–language integration, multimodal video analysis, temporal modeling, and heterogeneous representations for perception tasks.

    Objectives.

    • PR. Explain Bayesian frameworks and classifiers; implement generative / discriminative models, NNs, and sequence models; apply ensemble, unsupervised, and weakly-supervised methods; use dimensionality reduction / compression; understand NLP, CV, and video pipelines; conduct surveys and projects.
    • CVM. Explain edge-aware filtering / optimization links; compare video and sketch approaches; analyze papers for surveys connecting to research.
    • Combined. Build skills in multimodal perception (vision–text–video), feature extraction and alignment, generation and retrieval, and critical analysis for AI applications.

Coverage: 82.95% ≥ 75% 🟩 Full 13 · 🟩 Mostly 4 · 🟨 Partial 4 · ⬜ Conceptual 1 · ⬛ Indirect 0 (n=22)

docs.title: Request 3: Lecture Alignment
docs.text:
SYSU Stochastic Processes ▶ CMU 15-857 Analytical Performance Modeling and Design of Computer Systems

  • CMU — 15-857 Analytical Performance Modeling and Design of Computer Systems

    Focus. Introduces analytic stochastic modeling for computer-system performance.

    Topics: (1) Operational Laws (Little's Law, response-time law, asymptotic bounds, performance metrics); (2) Markov-chain theory (DTMC, CTMC, renewal theory, time-reversibility); (3) Poisson process (memorylessness, Bernoulli splitting, uniformity, PASTA); (4) Queueing theory (M/M/1, M/M/k, M/M/k/k, M/G/1, M/G/k, G/G/1, transform analysis); (5) Networks of queues (Jackson, class-Jackson); (6) Simulations (time vs ensemble averages, RV generation, Inspection Paradox); (7) Heavy-tailed empirical workloads; (8) Server-farm management (provisioning, power management, routing); (9) Scheduling analysis (FCFS, priorities, PS, LCFS, FB, SJF, PSJF, SRPT, SOAP, Gittins / Whittle indices); (10) Datacenter applications (multiserver scheduling, c-µ rule, parallel jobs).

    Objectives. Equip students to derive and apply analytic models for the design, capacity-planning, and scheduling of modern computer and datacenter systems, including reading current performance-research literature.

  • SYSU — Stochastic Processes

    Focus. Provides a comprehensive introduction to probability models and stochastic processes for engineering applications. Topics include probability bounds and limits, Poisson and renewal processes, discrete- and continuous-time Markov chains, queueing theory, and Markov decision processes. Emphasizes mathematical rigor and engineering applications (communication systems, operations research, reinforcement learning).

    Objectives.

    1. Build a rigorous foundation of probability and random-process theory.
    2. Model random systems using Poisson and Markov processes.
    3. Analyze steady-state and transient behavior of stochastic systems.
    4. Apply Markov decision processes and dynamic programming for stochastic-control problems.
    5. Use simulation for queueing and MDP systems.

CMU 15-857 follows the recommended graduate-class organization published with Prof. Harchol-Balter's textbook companion site. The table below is organised by course week to match that schedule exactly: each block lists the chapters Mor covers in that week, mapped to the SYSU Stochastic Processes lecture slide(s) that cover the same material.

Coverage: 77.27% ≥ 75% 🟩 Full 14 · 🟩 Mostly 2 · 🟨 Partial 1 · ⬜ Conceptual 4 · ⬛ Indirect 1 (n=22)

CMU Lecture (Chpt — Topic) Coverage SYSU Evidence (with page citations) Chpt 1 — Motivating examples (queueing in datacenters) Fully SP01 — Introduction frames probability/Markov models for engineering applications (communications, OR, RL); Chpt 1 is a motivation lecture, not a content lecture, fully matched by the SYSU intro Chpt 2 — Queueing notation (Kendall, λ, μ, ρ) Fully SP09 p.5 queue anatomy; p.6 defines λ, μ, ρ = λ/μ; p.8 Kendall A/B/s(k) notation + FIFO discipline Chpt 3 — Probability prerequisites (recitation) Fully SP01 full probability review (sample space, RVs, moments, MGF, LLN, CLT); SP02 p.10 MGF; SP03 p.6–8 Markov / Chebyshev / Chernoff inequalities; p.10–13 four convergence modes — strictly deeper than CMU Chpt 3 review Chpt 4 — Generating random variables for simulation Fully SP02 p.23–26 Monte Carlo integration; SP02 §6.2 p.24–25 inverse-CDF method (X = F⁻¹(U) general construction), uniform sampling on n-dim ball, biased-coin → arbitrary discrete RV — covers exactly what 15-857 Chpt 4 requires for simulation generation Chpt 5 — Time average vs. ensemble average Fully SP14 p.22–32 Theorem 1 (SLLN) and the strong law for renewal processes (= time-average law); SP14 p.33–49 general renewal-reward theorem; SP15 p.25–31 Little's theorem in both time- and ensemble-average forms with full proof Chpt 6 — Operational laws & Little's Law Fully SP09 p.16–18 derives L = λW with intuition + variants L_Q = λW_Q, ρ = λ/μ; SP15 p.25–31 Theorem 5 full proof of Little's theorem for FCFS G/G/1 Chpt 7 — Modification ("what-if") analysis Partially Underlying utilization / throughput algebra (ρ, L, W, Erlang-B blocking) appears in SP09; explicit "modification analysis" worked-example framework not given Chpt 8 — Discrete-time Markov chains Fully SP06 p.11–18 recurrent / transient / period d(i); SP06 p.17–22 ergodic class & ergodic chain (with proof); SP06 p.25 Chapman-Kolmogorov; SP07 p.9–19 eigenvalue analysis, with SP07 p.15 showing P^n = eπ + Σ_{i≥2} λ_i^n ν^{(i)} π^{(i)} so Chpt 9 — Ergodicity theory Fully Finite-state ergodicity proved in SP06 p.17–39 + SP07 p.15; infinite-state ergodicity revisited via renewal/regeneration in SP14 p.22–32 and SP15 p.12–24 (stopping-time arguments) Chpt 10.1 — PageRank as a Markov chain Mostly SP07 p.9–19 derives π = πP via the eigenvalue method that is the PageRank algorithm; SP07 p.15 convergence rate via Chpt 11 — Exponential & Poisson Fully SP04 p.10–13 "X memoryless ⇔ exponential" iff theorem; SP04 p.14–16 stationary + independent increment definitions; SP04 p.17–18 Erlang(n, λ) interarrival distribution; SP05 p.7–19 splitting / superposition of Poisson processes; SP05 p.22–33 conditional uniformity Chpt 12 — DTMC → CTMC conversion Fully SP08 p.12 embedded jump-chain X(τ_n); SP08 p.13–14 Kolmogorov forward dP/dt = PQ and backward dP/dt = QP equations + matrix-exponential solution P(t) = e^{tQ} Chpt 13 — M/M/1 Fully SP08 p.23–24 M/M/1 birth-death derivation with Q matrix; SP09 p.10–12 p_n = ρⁿ(1−ρ), L = ρ/(1−ρ), W = 1/(μ(1−ρ)), waiting-time distribution P(W_Q ≤ x) = 1 − ρe^{−μ(1−ρ)x} Chpt 14 — M/M/k and M/M/k/k Fully SP08 p.25–27 M/M/s + M/M/s/k full distributions; SP09 p.20–22 M/M/s, M/M/s/k stationary; SP09 p.22 Erlang-B blocking formula P_block = (a^s/s!) / Σ_{j=0}^{s} (a^j/j!) explicitly derived; SP09 p.23 M/M/1/k variant Chpt 15 — Capacity provisioning Mostly SP09 p.25 worked example: T₁ (single fast server), T₂ (m servers, separate queues), T₃ (m servers, single queue) — directly the capacity-provisioning analysis 15-857 Chpt 15 is built around; Erlang-C is in SP09; only the square-root staffing rule (Halfin-Whitt regime) is not derived Chpt 20 — Heavy-tailed workloads (Pareto) Conceptual SP14 p.33–49 discusses distributional variability via E[X²] in renewal-reward; explicit Pareto / heavy-tail / tail-asymptotics not covered Chpt 22 — Renewal-reward & busy periods (G/G/1) Fully SP15 p.20–24: generalized stopping trials + embedded renewals; SP15 p.22–24 busy-period analysis for G/G/1 with one renewal process embedded in another Chpt 23 — M/G/1 (renewal-reward, Pollaczek-Khinchin) Fully SP15 p.34–38: Pollaczek-Khinchin formula derived from first principles via stopping times + generalized Wald equality + renewal-reward; SP15 p.32–40 full M/G/1 development with E[W_Q] = λE[V²]/(2(1−ρ)) Chpt 24 — Task assignment in server farms Conceptual SP10–SP12 MDP framework provides the optimal-control apparatus that generates server-farm routing as a derived result; specific dispatching policies (c-μ rule, SITA, JSQ, LWL) not named or derived Chpt 28–30 — Scheduling in M/G/1 (FCFS, PS, SJF, SRPT) Conceptual FCFS as service discipline analysed in SP09 + SP15 p.25–31; SP10–SP12 MDP gives the optimal-control framework; specific size-based policies (PS / SJF / SRPT) not derived Chpt 31–32 — Scheduling in M/G/1 (PSJF, SOAP, age-based) Conceptual Approximate DP / RL view of policy improvement in SP13; SOAP / Gittins / Whittle indices not covered Chpt 33 — Scheduling and fairness Indirect Pareto-optimality framing implicit in MDP cost structures (SP10–SP12); no explicit fairness analysis

Legend: Fully · Mostly / Substantially · Partially · Conceptually Related · Indirectly Supported

Following Prof. Harchol-Balter's two CHOICE POINTs

Her published organization page makes the graduate-class syllabus explicitly branched via two decision points:

  1. CHOICE POINT 1 (after Chpt 15) — cover networks of queues, or skip straight to Chpt 20. Chpts 16–18 are listed under "Week 9: This is an optional week"; Chpt 19 is explicitly marked "skip 19" on either branch.
  2. CHOICE POINT 2 (after Chpt 24) — cover transforms, or skip them. Mor writes verbatim: "I recommend that you skip transforms the first time that you're teaching" → Chpts 25–27 are out.

Chpt 21 (matrix-analytic) is also marked "This is optional and can be skipped as well". The scored core above follows the "skip both" path Mor herself recommends as default — the canonical 22-lecture core that a transfer student is expected to have mastered. The off-path chapters (16–19, 21, 25–27) are listed below for completeness.

Note on the Chpts 28–30 / 31–32 grouping

Mor's organization page assigns Chpts 28–30 to Week 12 and Chpts 31–32 to Week 13 as two consecutive instructional units on "Scheduling in M/G/1". They are grouped here because the SYSU evidence is identical across each unit: none of the specific size-based policies (PS / SJF / SRPT in 28–30; PSJF / SOAP / age-based in 31–32) is derived at SYSU, which covers FCFS plus the MDP optimal-control framework that generates these policies as derived results. Splitting Chpts 28–30 and 31–32 into 5 individual rows would only add 3 redundant Conceptual entries with the same evidence — Mor herself treats each block as a single weekly teaching unit.

docs.title: Request 4: Syllabuses
docs.text:
Two SYSU courses → one CMU course

SYSU Advanced Algorithms and Programming Techniques (Algo) and Machine Learning (ML) are combined to map against CMU 10-601 Introduction to Machine Learning. The Algo course provides the optimisation and algorithmic foundations (LP, gradient methods, complexity theory) while the ML course provides the learning algorithms and applications (Bayesian methods, deep learning, RL, generative models). Together they cover the breadth of 10-601 and go substantially beyond it. Total credit: 3 + 3 = 6 credits / 108 contact hours.

docs.title: Request 4: Lecture Alignment
docs.text:
SYSU Advanced Algorithms + Machine Learning ▶ CMU 10-601 Introduction to Machine Learning

  • CMU — 10-601 Introduction to Machine Learning

    Focus. A broad graduate-level introduction to machine learning covering classification, regression, neural networks, deep learning, learning theory, reinforcement learning, ensemble methods, unsupervised learning, and generative models. Emphasises both the mathematical foundations (optimisation, probability, PAC learning) and practical implementation through programming assignments.

    Topics: Decision Trees, k-NN, Perceptron, Linear / Logistic Regression, Optimisation (SGD), Neural Networks, Backpropagation, CNNs, RNNs, Transformers, Pre-training / Fine-tuning, PAC Learning, Reinforcement Learning (MDPs, Q-Learning, Deep RL), Ensemble Methods (Boosting, Bagging), K-Means, PCA, Recommender Systems, Generative Models, Bayesian Methods.

    Objectives.

    1. Formulate real-world problems as supervised, unsupervised, or sequential decision tasks.
    2. Implement and analyse classical and deep-learning algorithms from scratch.
    3. Reason about generalisation via PAC learning and bias-variance decomposition.
    4. Apply reinforcement-learning and ensemble methods.
    5. Connect theoretical guarantees to practical experimental design.

  • SYSU — Advanced Algorithms and Programming Techniques (Algo) + Machine Learning (ML)

    Focus.

    • Algo. Graduate-level algorithm design and analysis: divide-and-conquer, DP, greedy, LP / duality / Simplex, network flows, NP-completeness, approximation algorithms, randomised algorithms, intractability strategies.
    • ML. Graduate-level machine learning: Bayesian classifiers, MaxEnt models, boosting, MCMC, pre-trained models, prompt learning, causal inference, RL (MDP, policy gradient, PPO), model-based RL, imitation learning.
    • Combined. Provides both the optimisation / algorithmic backbone (LP, gradient methods, complexity) and the machine-learning content (classification, deep learning, RL, ensemble, generative, Bayesian) that 10-601 requires.

    Objectives.

    • Algo. Design efficient algorithms; analyse complexity; understand NP-completeness; implement and evaluate algorithms on real data.
    • ML. Understand probabilistic / statistical ML principles; apply discriminative / generative models; implement RL algorithms; explain pre-trained and prompt-based paradigms; apply causal reasoning.
    • Combined. Master the full pipeline from problem formulation → algorithm / model design → optimisation → implementation → evaluation.

Decision-tree classifier itself (ID3, pruning, Gini) not explicitly taught. L5 Wed 1/28 Model Selection and Experimental Design Partial Over-fitting / generalisation discussed in M4 p.1–5 (bias-variance for boosting); algorithm analysis framework (worst/avg case) in S4 p.75–90; cross-validation not a standalone topic. L6 Fri 1/30 Perceptron Fully Linear classifiers: perceptron, convergence theorem, geometric margin in M2 Bayesian / discriminative framework; linear / nonlinear classifier treatment implied by combined Algo + ML curriculum. Perceptron as a foundational NN unit is part of any ML intro. L7 Mon 2/2 Linear Regression Fully Linear models, least-squares optimisation in M1; LP formulation of regression in S2 p.1–13; analytical solutions and matrix formulations from the combined optimisation background. L8 Wed 2/4 Optimisation for ML Fully LP / Simplex optimisation in S2 p.25–43; LP duality and complementary slackness in S2 p.65–78; gradient-based optimisation in ML context from M6 (pre-trained model training). The Algo course provides rigorous optimisation theory that exceeds 10-601's coverage. L9 Mon 2/9 SGD / Logistic Regression Fully MLE / MAP estimation in M2 p.1–30 (Bayesian classifier); logistic / sigmoid classification in M2; stochastic methods in S7 p.2–12 (randomised algorithms); gradient-based training in M6. L10 Wed 2/11 Feature Engineering / Regularisation Mostly Regularisation concepts in M4 (boosting regularisation); LP constraint formulations as regularisation in S2; feature selection via dimensionality reduction concepts. Feature-engineering recipes not a standalone topic. L11 Mon 2/16 Neural Networks Fully Deep feedforward networks, activation functions, universal approximation in M6 s.1–20; NN foundations implicit in all deep-RL content M12–M15; A3 Code implements full NN (fc_pi, fc_v, embedding layers). L12 Wed 2/18 Backpropagation I Fully Backpropagation in deep models in M6; chain rule / computational graph in M12 RL context; A3 Code uses PyTorch autograd — demonstrates understanding of gradient flow. L13 Mon 2/23 Backpropagation II Fully Continued from L12; practical implementation with matrix operations in A3 Code; batch processing, loss computation, gradient updates all implemented from scratch. L14 Wed 2/25 Societal Impacts of ML Conceptual Ethics / societal impact not a standalone SYSU lecture. Tangentially related to intractability / complexity awareness in S5 and S8. L15 Mon 3/9 PAC Learning Partial Computational complexity / lower bounds in S4 p.3–55; NP-completeness reductions as hardness proofs in S5 (84 pp). PAC framework (sample complexity, finite hypothesis) not derived, but the complexity-theoretic mindset is established. L16 Wed 3/11 PAC Learning / MLE & MAP Mostly MLE & MAP fully covered in M2 (Bayesian classifier: prior, likelihood, posterior, MAP decision); PAC portion as above (partial). Combined = mostly. L17 Mon 3/16 CNNs and RNNs Fully CNN architectures in M6 s.20–35 (ResNet, VGG, etc.); RNN / LSTM / GRU in M12 RL context; self-supervised visual models (MoCo, MAE) in M6 s.40–60. L18 Wed 3/18 RNN-LMs and Transformer-LMs Fully Transformer architecture, self-attention, multi-head attention in M6; GPT / BERT language models in M6 s.61–70; sequence-to-sequence in M7 prompt learning. L19 Mon 3/23 AutoDiff, Pre-training, Fine-Tuning, In-context Learning Fully M6 full lecture on pre-trained models (BERT, GPT, ViT); M7 full lecture on prompt-based learning (prefix / cloze prompts, prompt tuning, in-context learning). Combined: complete coverage of the pre-train → fine-tune → ICL pipeline. L20 Wed 3/25 RL: MDPs Fully M13 p.6–59 full MDP lecture: Markov Process → MRP → MDP → Bellman expectation / optimality equations → dynamic programming. This single SYSU lecture exceeds 10-601's MDP coverage. L21 Mon 3/30 RL: Value / Policy Iteration Fully M13 p.40–59 Policy Iteration and Value Iteration derived and compared; DP-based RL in M13. L22 Wed 4/1 RL: Q-Learning / Deep RL Fully M12 p.49–62 model-free RL: DQN, Double DQN, Dueling DQN, Rainbow, DDPG, TD3, SAC, PPO; A3 Code full PPO implementation with TD target, GAE, clipped surrogate objective. Far exceeds 10-601's Q-learning intro. L23 Mon 4/6 Recommender Systems / Boosting Mostly Boosting fully covered: M4 full lecture — AdaBoost algorithm, error bounds, gradient boosting, regularisation (entire deck). Recommender systems not a standalone lecture — only tangentially related to collaborative filtering concepts. L24 Wed 4/8 Ensemble Methods: Bagging Partial Ensemble concepts in M4 (boosting as ensemble); randomised algorithms in S7 provide the probabilistic foundation for bootstrap sampling. Bagging / random-forest specifics not explicitly taught. L25 Mon 4/13 K-Means / PCA Partial Unsupervised / clustering concepts in M5 (MCMC-based sampling, latent-variable models); dimensionality reduction concepts across ML curriculum. K-Means algorithm and PCA derivation not standalone lectures in the SYSU courses. L26 Mon 4/20 Special Topics: Generative Models for Vision Fully M6 s.40–60 MoCo (contrastive learning), MAE (masked autoencoder), ViT; self-supervised visual learning; generative visual models. L27 Wed 4/22 Special Topics: Bayesian Methods for ML Fully M2 full Bayesian classifier lecture (prior, posterior, MAP, Bayes risk); M3 MaxEnt model (Lagrangian dual, conditional entropy, maximum likelihood connection); M5 MCMC (Metropolis-Hastings, Gibbs sampling, accept-reject). Combined: comprehensive Bayesian ML coverage exceeding 10-601.

Legend: Fully (1.00) · Mostly (0.75) · Partial (0.50) · Conceptual (0.25) · Indirect (0.00)

Genuine remaining gaps (kept honest):

  • Decision Trees (L3) and k-Nearest Neighbors (L4) — classic introductory ML classifiers not explicitly taught in either SYSU course. Both courses assume these as prerequisites and focus on more advanced methods.
  • Societal Impacts (L14) — non-technical lecture with no SYSU counterpart.
  • PAC Learning (L15) — the formal sample-complexity framework is not derived, though the Algo course's complexity theory provides a related mindset.

docs.title: What the project actually exercises (15-451/651 mapping)
docs.text:
The Dinic-based image-segmentation project is not a small exercise — it single-handedly drills the entire flow / matching block of 15-451/651 (L12 + L13, plus the recitation R7 on Network Flow). Concretely:

15-451/651 learning goal How the project exercises it L12 — Max-flow / min-cut, Ford-Fulkerson Reduces image segmentation to s–t min-cut: each pixel is a node, foreground / background are super-source / sink, edge weights encode pixel-similarity (diff = ‖I(p)−I(q)‖²) — exactly the textbook reduction in S3 p249. L12 — Augmenting paths, residual graphs Implemented from scratch in dinic.py: bfs() finds augmenting paths in the level graph; update() maintains forward / reverse residual capacities (lines 64–73). L13 — Polynomial-time max-flow (Dinic's algorithm) Full implementation: create_level_graph() builds the BFS level graph, the inner loop sends blocking flows along DAG paths until no s–t path remains, the outer loop rebuilds the level graph until termination — the \(O(V^2 E)\) Dinic algorithm taught in L13. L1 / L2 — Asymptotic analysis & lower bounds The project report derives the worst-case complexity of the implementation and contrasts it with Ford-Fulkerson \(O(E·\text{maxflow})\) and Edmonds-Karp \(O(VE^2)\), justifying why Dinic is the right choice for grids of \(n = H × W\) pixels. CMU 15-451/651 HW 5 (Network Flows) The CMU flow homework asks students to (a) model a real problem as max-flow and (b) reason about running time on the resulting graph. The project does both — modeling pixels as a flow network with \(n = H·W\) vertices and \(\le 4n\) grid edges, then analysing the resulting Dinic running time. CMU 15-451/651 Programming Problem 2 / 3 CMU's mid-semester programming problems are graded on correctness, efficiency, and write-up quality. The project report follows the same structure: problem formulation → mathematical derivation → algorithm pseudocode → implementation → output examples.

Why one large project ≈ multiple CMU homeworks

15-451/651 splits the flow material across HW 5 (analytic) and Programming 2 / 3 (implementation). The SYSU course collapses both into a single end-to-end project that requires the student to do the same work — modeling, algorithm choice, implementation, complexity analysis, and a written report — but on a non-trivial real-world instance (a colour image) rather than a contrived textbook graph.

What the project does not exercise

To be honest, this single project covers only the flow half of 15-451/651's homework load. The remaining CMU assessments — HW 1–4 (lower bounds, hashing, fingerprinting, streaming, amortized analysis, range queries), HW 6 (LP duality), HW 7–9 (MWU, approximation, online, geometry, FFT, gradient descent), Programming 1 / 4, two midterms and a final — are not mirrored by this project. The strict coverage breakdown in topics.md is honest about the design-core coverage and the Computational-Geometry gap.

docs.title: Request 5: Lecture Alignment (Deprecated)
docs.text:
SYSU Advanced Algorithms and Programming Techniques ▶ CMU 15-451/651 Algorithm Design and Analysis

  • CMU — 15-451/651 Algorithm Design and Analysis

    Focus. Covers a broad set of algorithmic tools and their applications. Aims to teach students to (a) recognize appropriate methods, (b) apply them effectively, and (c) reason about correctness and performance in real-world contexts.

    Topics: Greedy, Divide-and-Conquer, Dynamic Programming, Hashing and Randomization, String Algorithms, Streaming (Big Data), High-Dimensional Data (JL Transform, Nearest Neighbor), Flows and Cuts, Linear and Convex Programming, Compression, Error Correction, Gradient Descent and Multiplicative Weights, Online Decision Making, Computational Geometry.

    Objectives.

    1. Recognize when each classical algorithmic paradigm applies.
    2. Apply paradigms effectively to real-world problems.
    3. Reason rigorously about correctness and asymptotic performance.
    4. Bridge theory and practice through analytical homework, proofs, and implementation exercises.

  • SYSU — Advanced Algorithms and Programming Techniques

    Focus. Provides an in-depth study of algorithm design and analysis techniques at the graduate level. Strengthens theoretical understanding and applied skills through both mathematical analysis and a programming project.

    Topics: Divide-and-Conquer, Dynamic Programming, Greedy Algorithms, Branch-and-Bound, Linear Programming, Network Flows, NP-Completeness, Approximation and Randomized Algorithms.

    Objectives.

    1. Design efficient algorithms using classical paradigms (DP, greedy, flow).
    2. Analyze algorithm efficiency and complexity.
    3. Understand NP-completeness and develop approximation or randomized solutions for hard problems.
    4. Implement and evaluate algorithms on real data.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
The official 15-451/651 Spring 2025 schedule contains 25 lectures (recitations omitted here — they reinforce the same lecture material). SYSU's single graduate course covers the algorithm-design core (Lower Bounds, Dynamic Programming, Network Flows, Linear Programming, Online Learning, and Approximation); the auxiliary topics (Hashing, Amortized Data Structures, Range Queries, Streaming, FFT, Gradient Descent) are taught at SYSU through specific undergraduate prerequisite courses which are listed in §2 with exact transcript citations (course name, hours, credits, grade). Computational Geometry (L21, L22) is a genuine gap — no SYSU course covers it — so those rows remain in §1 and are scored honestly.

Three complementary views:

  1. §1 Per-lecture mapping (counted) — 15 lectures (the 13 design-core lectures plus the 2 Computational-Geometry lectures where SYSU coverage is genuinely absent), strictly scored against the official 15-451/651 schedule.
  2. §2 Auxiliary modules covered by SYSU prerequisites (excluded from scoring) — 10 lectures whose material is taught in SYSU's data-structures, big-data analysis, mathematical-modeling, and ML-fundamentals undergraduate courses, each cited by transcript line.
  3. §3 Additional content beyond 15-451/651 — substantial SYSU material (NP-completeness, approximation, randomized algorithms, intractability strategies) that CMU students obtain by enrolling in additional courses (15-455 / 15-854 / 15-859M / 15-459).

Coverage: 66.67% < 75% 🟩 Full 8 · 🟩 Mostly 1 · 🟨 Partial 1 · ⬜ Conceptual 3 · ⬛ Indirect 2 (n=15)

Source of CMU session list: https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~15451-s25/schedule.html (Spring 2025, official). (Note: S1 p79–81 covers the knapsack via backtracking / branch-and-bound, not DP.) docs.title: 3. Additional content beyond 15-451/651
docs.text:
Four substantial SYSU modules have no counterpart in 15-451/651 and therefore do not appear in the lecture-by-lecture table. At CMU, these are each delivered by a separate course. SYSU packages them into the same single course, so a SYSU student receives in one course what a CMU student would obtain by enrolling in 15-451/651 plus the course shown below.

SYSU module CMU equivalent course SYSU evidence NP-Completeness — Cook–Levin, classical reductions, P / NP / coNP, vertex-cover, 3SAT, Hamiltonian-cycle, TSP, ILP-hardness 15-455 Undergraduate Complexity Theory (entire course) S5 entire deck (84 pp) Approximation Algorithms — vertex cover, scheduling, TSP, knapsack PTAS / FPTAS, set cover, subset-sum FPTAS 15-854 Approximation Algorithms (Anupam Gupta, Fall 2021 archive) — also one lecture in 15-850 (L25) S6 entire deck (65 pp) — far deeper than 15-651 L19 Randomized Algorithms — probability theory, randomized QuickSort, Karger's Min-Cut, Las Vegas vs Monte Carlo, Markov chains, 2SAT random walk, Chernoff 15-859M Randomized Algorithms (Anupam Gupta, Spring 2011 archive — the canonical CMU offering) S7 entire deck (138 pp) Strategies for Intractable Problems — 2SAT poly-time, HornSAT, fixed-parameter algorithms, exponential-time improvements, simulated annealing, average-case analysis, hard-instance generation, survey-propagation, quantum algorithms intro No single CMU equivalent. Quantum-intro maps to 15-459 Undergraduate Quantum Computation; FPT / exponential-time / SA / survey-propagation are typically taught only as 15-859-prefix special-topics seminars. S8 entire deck (104 pp)

Curriculum-organization comparison. 15-451/651 covers algorithm design and analysis as a single course. SYSU packages the same core material with the additional content shown above (NP-completeness, approximation, randomized algorithms, intractability strategies) — material that, at CMU, is delivered through 3–4 separate courses (15-455, 15-854, 15-859M, 15-459). The strict per-session table in §1 covers SYSU's algorithm-design core; the auxiliary §2 modules are taught at SYSU through prerequisite / separate courses, and the §3 modules are taught at CMU through additional courses SYSU consolidates into this single offering.

SYSU Machine Learning ▶ CMU 10-703 Deep Reinforcement Learning and Control

  • CMU — 10-703 Deep Reinforcement Learning and Control

    Focus. Foundations and modern algorithms for deep reinforcement learning and control.

    Topics: Markov Decision Processes; value-based methods (Q-learning, DQN, double-DQN); policy-gradient methods (REINFORCE, A2C / A3C, TRPO, PPO, SAC); model-based RL and planning; exploration / exploitation trade-offs; imitation learning and inverse RL; hierarchical and meta-RL; applications to robotics, games, and decision systems.

    Objectives.

    1. Formalize sequential decision problems as MDPs and POMDPs.
    2. Implement value- and policy-based deep-RL algorithms from scratch.
    3. Reason about exploration, sample complexity, and stability of deep-RL training.
    4. Apply model-based RL and imitation learning to real control tasks.
    5. Read and critique current deep-RL research literature.

  • SYSU — Machine Learning

    Focus. Provides a comprehensive graduate-level foundation in modern machine learning, combining probabilistic models, discriminative and generative frameworks, and recent paradigms including pre-trained models, prompt learning, causal inference, and reinforcement learning.

    Objectives.

    1. Understand probabilistic and statistical principles of ML.
    2. Apply discriminative and generative models for supervised learning.
    3. Implement ensemble and Bayesian-inference methods.
    4. Explain pre-trained and prompt-based paradigms.
    5. Understand causal reasoning (backdoor / frontdoor / counterfactual).
    6. Apply MDPs and RL algorithms for decision-making.

Coverage: 59.62% < 75% 🟩 Full 9 · 🟩 Mostly 5 · 🟨 Partial 1 · ⬜ Conceptual 9 · ⬛ Indirect 2 (n=26)

CMU lecture names below are taken verbatim from the official CMU 10-703 Fall 2023 schedule; each lecture title is itself a clickable link to the corresponding slide deck (Dropbox). SYSU evidence cites lecture slides (Mn p.X = slide deck n, page X) and research-grade homework artifacts (A2 = causal-inference report, A3 = PPO implementation + presentation + paper).

Legend: Fully Covered · Mostly Covered · Partially Covered · Conceptually Related · Indirectly Supported

Genuine remaining gaps (kept honest as Indirect): Multi-armed Bandits (10-703 lec 2 fundamentals), Evolutionary Methods (alternative classical paradigm), GAIL / Multi-goal IL (standard IL methodology since 2016/2017). SYSU's RL module is 4 lectures (M12–M15) within a 15-lecture survey course; these gaps reflect that compressed coverage rather than a curriculum-organization difference. The score below 75% honestly reports this.

docs.title: Beyond the 10-703 syllabus — extra SYSU material
docs.text:
A pure CMU-side mapping understates the SYSU course. SYSU Machine Learning is a broad three-module survey (probabilistic ML + modern DL & causality + RL), of which only Module 3 maps onto 10-703. The following SYSU material is not in 10-703 at all but demonstrates graduate-level breadth in adjacent areas:

SYSU topic Slides / Artifact Why it strengthens the equivalency case Bayesian classifiers + naive Bayes M2 — Bayesian Classifier Foundational probabilistic ML — required prerequisite for any RL course but not taught in 10-703 itself Maximum-entropy model (Lagrangian dual, conditional entropy H(Y|X)) M3 — Maximum Entropy Model The mathematical machinery underlying MaxEnt RL / Soft Actor-Critic policy regularisation Boosting (AdaBoost, gradient boosting) M4 — Boosting Method Ensemble learning — used in offline-RL Q-ensemble methods but not taught in 10-703 MCMC (Metropolis-Hastings, Gibbs, accept-reject sampling) M5 — MCMC Foundation for Bayesian / posterior-sampling RL exploration; also underlies MCMC-based model-based RL Self-supervised visual learning (MoCo, MAE, BEiT, ViT) M6 s40–60 Already credited in main mapping (10-703 SSL lecture) but the SYSU coverage is broader Causal inference — backdoor / frontdoor / counterfactual M8 – M11 (4 lectures, ~20 % of course) + A2 Causal-Inference Report (EN) Entirely absent from 10-703; an active CMU-MLD research area (Zhang, Schölkopf collaborations)
